title: Stable minimum principles for scattering states
abstract: Quantum-mechanical scattering states are energy eigenstates obeying particular boundary conditions, whose behavior at infinity encodes the S-matrix which defines the outcoming of scattering experiments. With an eye toward numerical algorithms for computing nonrelativistic S-matrices, we present a family of stable minimum principles for scattering states. States that approximately satisfy these minimum principles are shown to have a bounded difference with the true scattering states. These minimum principles and stability estimates can be used to obtain rigorous bounds on scattering amplitudes. We show that these minimum principles are applicable to momentum-dependent potentials, long-range (Coulomb) interactions, and elastic or inelastic scattering of bound states.
summary: The paper introduces a new family of stable minimum principles for approximating quantum scattering states using the L1 norm of the Schrodinger equation violation. It proves that this metric allows for rigorous error bounds on the S-matrix and cross sections, making it a robust foundation for numerical scattering algorithms. The method is shown to be applicable to complex systems, including momentum-dependent and many-body potentials.
main_problem: Developing stable variational minimum principles for approximating quantum-mechanical scattering states and bounding the error in the resulting S-matrix.
main_result: The authors establish that the L1 norm of the Schrodinger violation provides a stable metric for bounding errors in scattering amplitudes, phase shifts, and cross sections, unlike the L2 norm which can be unstable.
method: Introduction of a family of stable minimum principles using the L1 norm of the Schrodinger violation, designed to be compatible with modern computational algorithms like Neural Quantum States and Variational Monte Carlo.
model_or_system: Nonrelativistic quantum-mechanical scattering involving single-particle, many-body, and momentum-dependent potentials, including Coulomb interactions and scattering of bound states.
key_observables: S-matrix elements, scattering amplitudes, phase shifts, total cross section, and probability flux.
important_parameters: Energy (E), particle mass (M), potential strength and range, and the L1 norm of the Schrodinger violation.
main_assumptions: The approximate wavefunction is assumed to have the correct ingoing plane wave; potentials are generally assumed to be self-adjoint and decay sufficiently rapidly at large distances.
figures_summary: A schematic of the problem setup.
paper_structure: The paper presents the minimum principle, establishes stability estimates, extends results to general and momentum-dependent potentials, discusses many-body scattering, and provides counterexamples for unstable metrics.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and numerical physics as it provides a rigorous error-bounding framework for using variational/machine-learning-based wavefunctions to compute scattering observables.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
